text: Following the creation of pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon flips up the greater omentum and transverse colon to visually identify the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon dissects the transverse colon to expose the middle colic artery near its origin, then clips and cuts the artery.
The surgeon performs further dissection to expose the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, which is visibly entering the superior mesenteric vein from the right side.
The surgeon clips and cuts the gastric branch of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, exposing the middle colic vein, which is subsequently clipped and cut.
The surgeon performs dissection to expose the right gastroepiploic artery, ensuring careful preservation of the artery during the procedure.
The surgeon clips and cuts the colic branch of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, completing the dissection of this vascular structure.

The surgeon performs further dissection near the duodenal jejunal flexure, exposing the inferior mesenteric vein. The origin of the inferior mesenteric vein is divided to facilitate the upcoming takedown of the splenic flexure.
The preserved right gastroepiploic artery is visible alongside the inferior border of the pancreas, clearly demonstrated in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs further dissection to release the duodenal jejunal flexure, facilitating the mobilization of the transverse colon by taking down both the hepatic and splenic flexures.
The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament to expose the hepatic flexure, which is then taken down. The Cattle-brash maneuver is performed to reveal the duodenum.
The surgeon mobilizes the entire right hemicolon by dissecting along the white line of Toldt, separating the colon from the retroperitoneal attachments.
The surgeon divides the remaining gastrocolic ligament from the transverse mesocolon towards the left side, continuing the dissection to further mobilize the transverse colon.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac, revealing the posterior surface of the stomach, and continues dissection towards the left side of the colon.
The surgeon performs the Mattox maneuver to take down the splenic flexure, further mobilizing the transverse colon.
The specimen of the transverse colon is displayed, showing India ink tattooing marking the location of a stenosing tumor.
